solutions.solution: "That's all right, my dog ate your hen." "You'll have to pay for it, you rascal." Explanation: Here, A apologises to B that his hen destroyed by a dog. B readily answers in I that it was all right because this dog had eaten the hen. He says it as if there were an issue and it were settled with the hen being eaten. This is funny if we try to imagine A's face when he hears the answer. Reply II is not funny as it is the most common response to such a news.

Sometimes an incident may be funny because of a twisted use of a word into a different meaning. Ex 11: "That guy is one of the greatest half-backs that ever lived. He always pays back half of what he borrows." "was an asset for his club team!" Explanation: I is funny as it twists the meaning of the word half-back. Usually, half-back is a term associated with football. But here it is used in the sense of "one who pays back half of what he borrows". This twisted use makes it funny. II of course, is not funny as it is in the tone of the mood of respect in the opening part of the sentence creates. Conclusion: There are many ways in which an incident can be funny. In the foregoing pages, we have tried to enlist some of them. But we cannot produce a list of all the possible cases because their number is infinite. But it won't be too difficult for you to detect a funny incident if you learn to appreciate the basic idea. Instead of doing a full-fledged post-mortem, I would suggest a simple method: Read the thing; if it makes you smile, it is funny; if it doesn't please move to a new.

A.III: It must have a sudden twist and the twist should be into something not expected The third and final criterion for a suggested statement to fit as a punchline is that it must have a sudden twist so that it brings about something not expected. The keyword here is twist into something not expected. So, there must be an element of surprise in it. Now, detecting whether a given statement has a sudden twist or not, is a very easy job. If there is a sudden twist, you will automatically notice it. When there is an element of surprise, you will automatically be surprised. The following few examples show how a surprising twist is brought about in statements. Ex 12: When a man talks enthusiastically about hard work, the chances are: I. that he is an employer. II. that he is just another loudmouth.

"That article was so boring it sent me to sleep." Ex.13: "What did it discuss?" A: "——" I. Insomnia. II. Drug abuse and its psychosomatic after-effects. Explanation: I reply I has an element of surprise in it. Insomnia is a disease which means a lack of sleep. Now, A has already mentioned that he was bored and put to sleep by reading the article. It was completely unexpected that an article which puts him to sleep would be talking about insomnia! Therefore, I passes as a punchline as it does not. When we read "Drug abuse and its psychosomatic after-effects", we find neither funny nor surprised.

Ex 14: "When I stand on my head, the blood rushes to it. Why doesn't it rush to my feet now?" B: "——" I. Because your feet aren't empty. II. You should get yourself examined by a doctor. Explanation: Here, reply I has a sudden twist into an unexpected answer. A makes a query which looks a normal example of human curiosity. But B twists. A's statement. When he says "Because your feet aren't empty", it implies that A's head is empty. This is an unexpected twist. Reply II has no such twist; most people would say a similar thing in such circumstances.

Ex 15: Doctor: "You'll be pleased to hear that the operation you had for deafness has been a complete success." Patient: "——" I. "I beg your pardon?" II. "Let us see if I am equally pleased to hear about the bill." Explanation: In this question, both reply I and reply II have that sudden twist. When the doctor breaks the news of successful operation, we normally expect people to say things like "Oh, thank you, Doctor!" or "That's great. I can hear now." But both the replies here are unexpected. Reply I implies that
